en.extract:
The brown-necked parrot (Poicephalus fuscicollis), sometimes known in aviculture as the uncape parrot, is a large Poicephalus parrot species endemic to Africa. 
It consists of two subspecies: the savanna-dwelling brown-necked parrot (P. fuscicollis fuscicollis) and grey-headed parrot (P. f. suahelicus) subspecies. It formerly included the Cape parrot (now Poicephalus robustus) as a subspecies before the Cape parrot was re-classified as a distinct species.


Taxonomy
German naturalist Heinrich Kuhl described the brown-necked parrot in his 1819 work Conspectus Psittacorum. Although unsure of its country of origin, he felt it was definitely a distinct species and related to the Cape parrot. The species name is from the Latin words fuscus "dark" and collum "neck".
South Africa-based ornithologist Phillip Clancey proposed the Cape and brown-necked parrots were separate species in 1997 based on the shape and size of the bill, head coloration and preferred habitat. Mike Perrin observed that species status would facilitate protection of the endangered Cape parrot. Genetic analysis of the three taxa published in 2015 supported the distinctness of brown-necked and cape parrots, showing that ancestors of the two had diverged between 2.13 and 2.67 million years ago—in the late Pliocene to early Pleistocene epoch. This period was a period of changes in climate, where grassland and forest were expanding and contracting, which led to isolation and eventually speciation of separate populations.
The old name for the three taxa was Cape parrot, with virtually all the individuals in captivity belonging to what became P. fuscicollis. Jean Pattison called them Uncape parrots because of this.


Description
The largest member of its genus, the brown-necked parrot has a relatively large head and bill, and stocky build. It has a light grey head. The nominate subspecies fuscicollis has a bluer sheen to its plumage than suahelicus. The upperparts are yellow-green and underparts greenish.


Distribution
Subspecies fuscicollis is found in west Africa from Gambia and southern Senegal to Ghana and Togo. Locally common in places, it appears to have declined in Senegal and Gambia. Subspecies suahelicus is found in southern Africa from southern Congo and Tanzania to northern Namibia and south to northern South Africa.


Aviculture
The species adapts readily to captivity and is seen in the pet trade.


References


External links
 Media related to Poicephalus fuscicollis at Wikimedia Commons

(''Poicephalus fuscicollis suahelicus'') | genus = Poicephalus | species = fuscicollis | authority = ([[Heinrich Kuhl|Kuhl]], 1820) | range_map = | range_map_caption = }} The '''brown-necked parrot''' ('''''Poicephalus fuscicollis'''''), sometimes known in aviculture as the '''uncape parrot''',{{cite web|last1=Harris|first1=Craig|title=Uncape Parrot birds|url=http://www.birdchannel.com/bird-species/profiles/uncape-parrot-2.aspx|website=BirdChannel|access-date=28 August 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140904205703/http://www.birdchannel.com/bird-species/profiles/uncape-parrot-2.aspx|archive-date=4 September 2014}} is a large [[Poicephalus|''Poicephalus'' parrot]] species endemic to Africa. It consists of two subspecies: the savanna-dwelling '''brown-necked parrot''' (''P. fuscicollis fuscicollis'') and '''grey-headed parrot''' (''P. f. suahelicus'') subspecies. It formerly included the [[Cape parrot]] (now ''Poicephalus robustus'') as a subspecies before the Cape parrot was re-classified as a distinct species. ==Taxonomy== German naturalist [[Heinrich Kuhl]] described the brown-necked [[parrot]] in his 1819 work ''Conspectus Psittacorum''. Although unsure of its country of origin, he felt it was definitely a distinct species and related to the Cape parrot.{{cite book | author= Kuhl, Heinrich | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36827601 | year =1819 |page=93 |publisher=self-published | location= Bonn, Germany | title=Conspectus Psittacorum : cum specierum definitionibus, novarum descriptionibus, synonymis et circa patriam singularum naturalem adversariis, adjecto indice museorum, ubi earum artificiosae exuviae servantur: cum tabulis III. aeneis pictis }} The species name is from the [[Latin]] words ''fuscus'' "dark" and ''collum'' "neck".{{cite book |author=Simpson, D.P. |title=Cassell's Latin Dictionary |publisher=Cassell |year=1979 |orig-date=1959 |edition=5 |location=London, United Kingdom |pages=116, 261 |isbn=0-304-52257-0}} South Africa-based ornithologist [[Phillip Clancey]] proposed the Cape and brown-necked parrots were separate species in 1997 based on the shape and size of the bill, head coloration and preferred habitat. Mike Perrin observed that species status would facilitate protection of the endangered Cape parrot.Perrin, M.R. 2005. A review of the taxonomic status and biology of the Cape Parrot ''Poicephalus robustus'', with reference to the Brown-headed Parrot ''P. fuscicollis fuscicollis'' and the Grey-headed Parrot ''P. f. suahelicus''. Ostrich 76: 195–205 [[Genetic analysis]] of the three [[taxon|taxa]] published in 2015 supported the distinctness of brown-necked and cape parrots, showing that ancestors of the two had diverged between 2.13 and 2.67 million years ago—in the late [[Pliocene]] to early [[Pleistocene]] epoch. This period was a period of changes in climate, where grassland and forest were expanding and contracting, which led to isolation and eventually speciation of separate populations.{{cite journal |vauthors=Coetzer WG, Downs CT, Perrin MR, Willows-Munro S |year=2015|title= Molecular Systematics of the Cape Parrot (''Poicephalus robustus''): Implications for Taxonomy and Conservation|journal= PLOS ONE |volume=10|issue=8| doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0133376 |pmid=26267261 |pmc=4534405 |article-number=e0133376|doi-access=free|bibcode=2015PLoSO..1033376C }} The old name for the three taxa was Cape parrot, with virtually all the individuals in captivity belonging to what became ''P. fuscicollis''. Jean Pattison called them Uncape parrots because of this.{{cite book|author=Mattie Sue Athan|author2=Dianalee Deter|title=Guide to the Senegal Parrot and Its Family|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FcCEN6VOdjMC&pg=PA15 |year=2008|publisher=Barron's Educational Series|isbn=978-0-7641-3886-7|pages=15–16}} ==Description== The largest member of its genus, the brown-necked parrot has a relatively large head and bill, and stocky build. It has a light grey head. The nominate subspecies ''fuscicollis'' has a bluer sheen to its plumage than ''suahelicus''.{{Cite book |first=Joseph M. |last=Forshaw | author-link=Joseph Forshaw |title=Parrots of the World |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |isbn=978-1-4008-3620-8|page= 148 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nImgkTTCQrsC&pg=PA148| year=2010}} The upperparts are yellow-green and underparts greenish. [[File:Grey-headed parrot.jpg|thumb|left|Juvenile grey-headed parrot]] ==Distribution== Subspecies ''fuscicollis'' is found in west Africa from Gambia and southern Senegal to Ghana and Togo. Locally common in places, it appears to have declined in Senegal and Gambia.{{cite book |title=A Field Guide to birds of The Gambia and Senegal |last= Barlow |first=Clive |author2=Wacher, Tim |author3=Disley, Tony |publisher=Yale University Press|year=2005|page=226 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1vHyDfF3vDUC&pg=PA226 |isbn= 978-0-300-11574-1}} Subspecies ''suahelicus'' is found in southern Africa from southern Congo and Tanzania to northern Namibia and south to northern South Africa. ==Aviculture== The species adapts readily to captivity and is seen in the pet trade. ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline|Poicephalus fuscicollis|''Poicephalus fuscicollis''}} {{Psittacinae}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1543843}} [[Category:Poicephalus|brown-necked parrot]] [[Category:Birds of Sub-Saharan Africa]] [[Category:Birds described in 1820|brown-necked parrot]]
